competitors.label: MDN Callback function
competitors.selectionReason: MDN's callback glossary page is the canonical docs baseline for this concept and a likely comparator for simple callback definitions.
competitors.winReason: Our page now goes beyond the glossary definition by pairing inversion-of-control framing with a DOM-event example and a callback-to-Promise refactor, while MDN remains intentionally brief.
theirStrengths: It provides the authoritative baseline definition very concisely.
theirGaps: It is intentionally brief and does not push far into production-debug tradeoffs.

realWorldUseCases: A callback is a function you hand to another API so it can decide when to call you. The real production/debug issue is inversion of control: DOM events, file APIs, or legacy libraries own the timing, the error path, and whether the work can be canceled or composed cleanly.
MDN defines callbacks correctly, but it stays closer to a glossary baseline than to inversion-of-control tradeoffs in real UI or legacy-async code.
actionableExamples: Its concise examples are useful, but they are smaller than our DOM-event example plus the callback-to-Promise before/after flow.
followUpConfusion: MDN still explains the basic callback idea clearly, so the broader follow-up set is close rather than clearly behind.
Add one short note on cancellation or cleanup concerns in callback-style APIs compared with AbortSignal-aware Promise APIs.

competitors.label: javascript.info Callbacks
competitors.selectionReason: javascript.info has a dedicated tutorial on callbacks with concrete async examples, making it a strong tutorial comparator.
competitors.winReason: javascript.info has a strong async loader walkthrough, but our page now wins this interview-sized comparison by combining a before/after callback-to-Promise refactor with the repeated-notification follow-up that explains when callbacks still fit.
theirStrengths: It walks through a realistic async callback example and then shows how the pattern leads toward promises.
theirGaps: It is longer and less directly interview-oriented than our trivia format.

followUpConfusion: Promises settle once; callbacks can be called many times.

realWorldUseCases: javascript.info still has a very strong single real-world async scenario, so the real-world criterion stays close.
actionableExamples: Its loadScript example is concrete, but our page now adds both nested-callback pain and the Promise-based cleanup path.
followUpConfusion: Our page now answers the key follow-up of when callbacks remain the right tool more directly.

realWorldUseCases: Add one callback cleanup or unsubscribe example if we want a clearer edge on lifecycle-heavy real-world flows.

competitors.label: GreatFrontEnd Callback functions
competitors.selectionReason: GreatFrontEnd has a directly relevant interview-prep answer for callback functions.
competitors.winReason: Our page is now stronger for this topic because it frames callbacks as an inversion-of-control debugging problem, then shows both an event-driven callback and a callback-to-Promise refactor.
theirStrengths: It is aligned with the interview intent and stays on the callback concept without too much unrelated detail.
theirGaps: It appears lighter on the inversion-of-control tradeoff than our current framing.

realWorldUseCases: A callback is a function you hand to another API so it can decide when to call you. The real production/debug issue is inversion of control: DOM events, file APIs, or legacy libraries own the timing, the error path, and whether the work can be canceled or composed cleanly.
GreatFrontEnd explains where callbacks are used, but it is lighter on the concrete inversion-of-control debugging angle.
actionableExamples: Its examples are concise, while our page now includes both an event callback and a before/after Promise refactor.
followUpConfusion: Its interview-prep framing already covers the baseline follow-up, so the comparison remains close rather than clearly behind.
Add one tiny cleanup example that removes an event listener or unsubscribes a callback to strengthen the lifecycle follow-up.
